Until now, rigorous bounds for Laplace expansions in growing dimension have been restricted to the “Gaussian-approximation” regime, known to hold when d2/λ → 0. This excludes many practically relevant regimes, including those arising in physics and modern high-dimensional statistics, which operate beyond this threshold while still satisfying the concentration condition d/λ → 0. When λ is large, these integrals are dominated by neighborhoods of minimizers of f, and Laplace asymptotics yields explicit leading-order formulas and systematic higher-order corrections. Such approximations underpin both rigorous analysis (e.g. sharp tail probabilities and free-energy ex- pansions) and practical computation in settings where direct numerical integration is infeasible. In the classical regime where the dimension d is fixed and λ → ∞, Laplace’s method and its higher-order refinements are well developed [4, Section 8.3], [44, Section 5, Chapter IX]. At the opposite extreme, there is also an infinite-dimensional theory (e.g. on Wiener space) in which λ−1 plays the role of a small-noise parameter [2]. What remains comparatively less understood is the intermediate regime in which the dimension grows with the large parameter. This “growing-d” regime is now very common in modern statistics, where Laplace-type integrals (or ratios thereof) appear as marginal likelihoods and posterior expectations in models whose parameter dimension increases with sample size. See Section 1.2 for more details. Likewise, the absence of rigorously justified “growing-d” expansions created a significant void in areas such as statistical physics, Euclidean quantum field theories (QFT), and chemistry, where formal expansions have been in use for a long time without adequate justification, see Section 1.1. Recent work by the second author establishes a high-dimensional Laplace expansion of I(λ), with explicit remainder control, under the scaling d2/λ → 0 [22]. The proof centers around an approximation of f in the exponent by its second-order Taylor expansion. Thus the expansion of I(λ) is closely tied to the problem of approximating concentrating densities, of the form π ∝ e−λf, by Gaussian distributions. In fact, the condition d2 ≪ λ arises in numerous contexts as a threshold for Gaussian approximation accuracy. In the context of Gaussian approximation to posteriors in Bayesianinference,d2 ≪ λappearsintheworks[21,20,17,23]. Theconditiond2 ≪ λalsoarisesin proofs of high-dimensional Central Limit Theorems (CLTs); see [32, 46, 19]. In the CLT, λ (usually denoted n) is the given number of i.i.d. d-dimensional random vectors, whose average is of interest. In fact, the aforementioned works [22, 21, 32] establish lower bounds as well, proving d2/λ ≪ 1 is necessary for the Gaussian approximations to be accurate. Although some works have in fact proved the CLT under much weaker conditions on d relative to λ, the Gaussian approximation holds in a much weaker sense. See Section 3.1 and Remark 2 of [8] for an overview of this line of work. Thus to summarize, the regime d2/λ → 0 arises as a critical threshold in numerous results on approximating d-dimensional densities or integrals involving e−λf. Another critical threshold is the regime d/λ → 0, which is generically required for distributions ∝ e−λf to concentrate near the minimizer of f; see e.g. [12, Corollary 2.1] and [38, Section 4.1]. Clearly, if there is no concentration aroundtheminimizeroff, oneshouldnotexpectanexpansiontoholdwhosetermsaredetermined solely by the derivatives of g and f at this single point. This leaves an intriguing intermediate region where d2/λ does not vanish or even diverges to infinity, while d/λ still converges to zero. Due to concentration about the minimizer of f, one expects that I(λ) in (1.1) can still be characterized by derivatives of f and g at the minimizer. But it is unclear how to obtain a closed form approximation to the integral without leaning on the tractable Gaussian integral obtained by replacing f with its second-order Taylor expansion. To our knowledge, there has been no rigorously justified explicit Laplace-type approximation of I(λ) in this intermediate regime. In this paper we have achieved an important milestone by completely characterizing this largely unexplored intermediate regime under natural local regularity and global growth conditions. We derive an explicit asymptotic series approximating (1.1) such that for each expansion order L ≥ 1, the remainder is negligible as long as dL+1/λL → 0. The series approximates the logarithm of the integral I(λ), and as we explain below, this is √ precisely what allows us to push d above the λ barrier. Let x be the minimizer of f. Assuming ⋆ 2
boundedoperatornormsofderivativesoff andg nearx throughorders2L+2and2L,respectively, ⋆ and mild global growth of f away from x , we show that ⋆ L−1 (cid:88) logI(λ) = b (f,g)λ−k +O(dL+1/λL) (1.2) k k=1 for some coefficients b (f,g) that satisfy b (f,g) = O(dk+1). If d ≲ logλ, then additional factors of k k logλ are present in the remainder. The sum with respect to k is omitted if L = 1, and we assumed without loss of generality that g = 1 at x . We prove that the coefficients b can be derived from ⋆ k formal (and well-known) cumulant expansions. Significance. A central significance of our result is that it advances the modern asymptotic anal- ysis program of developing expansions of ubiquitous Laplace type integrals that remain accurate in increasingly high-dimensional regimes. The best known results to date work under the assumption d2/λ → 0. In contrast, we extend the range of dimensions for which one can make precise asymp- totic statements with explicit formulas until the very limit, because beyond d/λ → 0 there is no concentration any longer. In this sense, our work brings the program initiated by Laplace in the 1700s to its logical conclusion, by providing explicit asymptotics (with remainder control) up to the threshold where Laplace-type concentration breaks down. We show that a simple analytic approx- imation remains valid whenever dL+1/λL → 0 for any L ≥ 1, even in the genuinely intermediate region where d2/λ ̸→ 0 (indeed, where d2/λ may diverge), by identifying an explicit exponential correction at the level of the log-integral. Theimplicationsofourresultsacrossmanyareasofscienceandengineeringarenumerous. Two particularly noteworthy applications deserve special mention. Thefirstapplicationisinphysics, includingstatisticalphysicsandQFT,whereLaplace-typein- tegrals encode quantities of central importance. In the high-dimensional, many-degrees-of-freedom regimes relevant to these fields, such quantities are often evaluated via formal Laplace expansions, typically without remainder bounds that remain valid in the joint limit of a large parameter and growing dimension; see Section 1.1 for details. This theoretical gap, which dates back at least to the Darwin–Fowler steepest-descent approach in 1922 [9], has long limited the rigor of many computations. Our results fill the century-old gap by placing these Laplace calculations on firm mathematical footing for a broad class of finite-dimensional large-system models. Another area where our results are of significant importance is statistics. Here, λ plays the role of sample size. Our results address the need of modern statistics to develop rigorously-justified Laplace asymptotics that remain accurate in increasingly high-dimensional regimes. Indeed, high- dimensional Laplace integrals are ubiquitous in statistics, arising as marginal likelihoods, model ev- idences, or other posterior expectations. Complicating matters is the fact that these integrals must often be evaluated repeatedly for different parameter choices, and then optimized over. Laplace approximations such as the Bayesian Information Criterion are popular and extremely convenient because they produce explicit approximations rather than black-box numerical estimates. Yet until now, theoretical backing for these approximations was limited to the d2/λ → 0 regime. Our results push these guarantees into substantially higher-dimensional regimes, arbitrarily close to d/λ → 0. See Section 1.2 for more details and references. Besides physics and statistics, high-dimensional Laplace-type integrals arise throughout sci- ence and engineering, including in molecular simulation and theoretical chemistry (e.g., partition functions and free-energy calculations) [42, 25], Bayesian inverse problems [13], and others. Finally, our result may also be of interest in the theory of cumulants, since it solves the problem of bounding the remainder in cumulant expansions. Obtaining a remainder bound using cumulant 3
theory (and the closely related theory of Gaussian chaos [15, 31, 30]) directly is deeply nontrivial. We have avoided this problem by finding an alternative route. In field-theoretic language, the terms in (1.8) are precisely the loop corrections to mean field: Wick expansion of the Gaussian moments [16, Sections 5.1], [47, Section 1.1] produces a sum over Feynman diagrams, and the cumulant picks out the 4

Physics literature makes extensive use of truncations of (1.8) (or equivalent loop expansions). However, derivations are usually done without rigorous error control [16, Sections 2.1 and 2.2] and [6, Sections 1.1–1.3]. For finite d, the error control follows from classical asymptotic analysis [4, 44]. For growing d, such expansions have never been justified except for some specific cases. The scaling condition dL+1/λL → 0, d,λ → ∞ is exactly the statement that (L−1)-loop-corrected mean field has a provable accuracy guarantee in a growing- system regime. From a physics viewpoint, (1.9) can be read as supplying the missing “error bars” for a procedure that is otherwise typically justified heuristically (or by numerics), thereby turning the loop expansion into a rigorously justified approximation scheme in regimes where the effective number of degrees of freedom grows with the large parameter in the exponential. 1.2 Application in statistics Laplace-type integrals (cid:82) g(x)e−nf(x)dx are omnipresent in statistics, with n = λ typically playing Rd the role of sample size, and f depending weakly on n [40]. These tasks become particularly demanding in the growing- d regimes that now arise routinely in applications. For example, [14] tackles the above Laplace optimization problem in the context of deep learning, where the integration is over the space of hundreds of parameters of a neural network. In this setting of optimizing an objective given by a high-dimensional integral, Laplace approxi- mations are especially attractive. Indeed, they yield an explicit analytic surrogate for the objective as a function of the tuning parameter, rather than the black-box numerical access provided by methods such as Markov Chain Monte Carlo [33]. However, this convenience is only meaningful when the approximation error is controlled in a dimension-dependent way. In fact, concerns about the reliability of the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) in high dimensions have been noted ex- plicitly in the literature. The BIC is a further approximation on top of the Laplace approximation used to simplify the optimization objective. In [10, p. 377], the authors write “for models with a large number of predictors [large d] it is no longer clear that some version of the BIC, or perhaps rather a Laplace approximation, accurately approximates a marginal likelihood”. While recent work has begun to provide rigorous guarantees for high-dimensional Laplace ap- proximations[37,3,39,22],thesetypicallyrequiremorerestrictivescalings(atbest,thatd2/λ ≪ 1). Our results push these guarantees essentially up to the concentration boundary, providing explicit “error bars” for Laplace-based surrogates deep into the modern high-dimensional regime. 1.3 Connection to cumulant theory As stated above, the coefficients b in (1.2) can be derived from formal cumulant expansions. k Although computing the coefficients using cumulants is easy, bounding the remainder in the ex- pansion of logI(λ) using cumulant theory seems deeply nontrivial. Indeed, cumulants have been thoroughly studied in the statistics, physics and combinatorics literature, yet rigorous remainder bounds on cumulant expansions in the growing d regime are virtually non-existent. This sug- gests that bounding the remainder through the lens of cumulants may be intractable. In contrast, our change-of-variables approach is not only tractable, but also avoids any heavy machinery, as mentioned above. Our remainder bound makes rigorous the well-known observation that, based on the terms, expanding the cumulant generating function (cgf) is advantageous to expanding the moment gen- erating function (mgf). Specifically, it is well-known that each cumulant is given by a sum of a fewer number of summands than the corresponding moment. For example at the end of Section 3.10.1 of [26], McCullagh writes that the formula for cumulants is a sum only over “connected pairs” of bi-partitions, as opposed to the formula for moments. In [16, Chapter 5.3], the author writes, “When calculating cumulants, only fully connected diagrams (without disjoint pieces) need to be included. This is a tremendous simplification.” The simplification due to this summing over fewer terms in fact leads to a substantial improve- ment. By analyzing the cumulants and moments directly in our setting, it is possible to show that the moments that contribute to the kth coefficient in the I(λ) expansion are generically of order O(d2k), while the cumulants that contribute to the kth coefficient in the logI(λ) expansion are generically of order O(dk+1). Of course, this analysis of the terms does not rigorously prove any- thing about the remainders. However, it naturally leads to the hypothesis that the remainders of 6
the two expansions behave like the first terms dropped from the truncation, i.e. that the Lth order remainders scale as O(d2L/λL) and O(dL+1/λL), respectively. This is precisely what the previous work [22] and the present paper rigorously prove. Thus,asmentionedabove,expandingthelogarithmofI(λ)iswhatallowsustorelaxthed2 ≪ λ requirement in [22]. Given our result (1.2), it is very easy to see why d2 ≪ λ is necessary for an additive expansion of I(λ) to any order. Indeed, we have shown in (1.2) that, to leading order, I(λ) = exp(O(d2/λ)). Thus if we Taylor expand the exponential to obtain an additive expansion of I(λ), powers of d2/λ are present throughout. There is no finite number of terms we can subtract from I(λ) to remove the dependence on d2/λ. However, we can remove the dependence on d2/λ simply by dividing I(λ) by the single term exp(b (f,g)/λ). The multiplicative remainder is then 1 of order exp(O(d3/λ2)). In Section 8, we illustrate the power of expanding logI(λ) in two concrete examples: a quar- tic perturbation of a Gaussian exponent, and a logistic-regression-type likelihood motivated by statistics. 1.4 Related work There is a related body of work on Laplace-type integrals arising in statistics (e.g. posterior nor- malizing constants and marginals) and statistical physics (partition functions) in the “proportional asymptotics” regime. Here, d/λ converges to a constant. The regime lies beyond the concen- tration threshold, so these integrals can only formally be considered to be Laplace-type. Due to the lack of concentration, entirely different techniques are required to study such asymptotics. See [24, 35, 7, 27, 28], as well as the referenced cited therein. Organization The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces the notation and conventions used through- out. In Section 3, we describe the problem setting and state the main result, and Section 4 outlines the proof strategy. Section 5 derives the expansion of the main contribution to the integral coming from a neighborhood of zero, while Section 6 computes the expansion coefficients in terms of cu- mulants. The tail contribution is estimated in Section 7. Section 8 compares our results with the expansion of I(λ) in [22] and presents two applications of our expansion. sections.section_title: 2. As can be seen from [22], d2 ≪ λ is necessary for the expansion of I(λ) even when the derivative operator norms are bounded. Indeed, consider the example in Section 8.2, also studied in Example 2.19 in [22]. We see that the derivative operator norms are indeed bounded, yet [22] proves that the expansion of I(λ) is valid only if d2 ≪ λ. This shows that our improved dimension dependence cannot simply be attributed to the stricter requirement we have imposed on the operator norms. Rather, it is due to the intrinsic difference between expanding I(λ) and expanding logI(λ), as described in the introduction. 3. In the expansion of I(λ) in [22], the derivative operator norms beyond the fourth order were allowed to grow with d due to slack in the bound. A similar phenomenon may hold for the expansion of logI(λ). Namely, there may be some slack which would permit derivative norm growth with d. We leave this investigation to future work. 8.2 Example: quartic exponent Let f(x) = ∥x∥2/2 + ∥x∥4/24 and g(x) ≡ 1. Consider (3.2) and (3.3).
